module.title: Module 5: Locating Simultaneous Conveyances
module.description: How surveyors and courts locate boundaries created at the same legal instant — the lots of a recorded subdivision plat — where all parcels are of equal dignity, found original monuments control, and any excess or deficiency is apportioned proportionately rather than absorbed by a junior parcel.
learning_objectives: Identify the process by which boundaries are simultaneously created and the three requirements for a simultaneous conveyance.
Apply the order of controlling evidence (found original monuments, then plat record, then proportion) for locating lots in a subdivision.
Apply proportionate (apportionment) measurement to distribute excess and deficiency among lots in proportion to their record dimensions.
Apply rules of construction for deed interpretation in combined conveyances and 'part-of-a-lot' descriptions.

lessons.title: What Makes a Conveyance Simultaneous
learning_objectives: Define a simultaneously created boundary and list the three requirements that establish one.
Explain why simultaneously created parcels are of equal dignity and have no senior rights among themselves.
Recognize other transactions (partition decrees, devises by will) that are treated as simultaneous conveyances.

content.html:
Created in the same legal instant

A simultaneously created boundary results when several parcels of land are created in the same legal instant, by the same person, persons, or agency, and by the same instrument. The classic example is a recorded subdivision plat: when the map is approved and recorded and lots are sold by reference to it, every lot springs into existence at once. Because they are born together, all parcels have equal standing — no lot can claim prior rights or seniority over any other. This is the fundamental difference from the sequential conveyances of Module 4, where parcels are carved off one at a time and senior/junior rights govern.

The three requirements

For a simultaneous-conveyance situation to exist, the threshold requirement is that two or more parcels are created from a single parent parcel at one time. Brown states the recorded-plat case as three steps:

  • 1. All divisions of the land are indicated and shown on a plat or map.
  • 2. The map is filed and approved.
  • 3. Parcels are sold with reference to the approved plat or map.

When those conditions are met, the order in which the individual lots later sell is immaterial to seniority among them. A lot sold first is not senior to a lot sold last.

content.text: Equal dignity, but unwritten rights still prevail. Excesses and deficiencies in a subdivision are proportioned according to the frontage of each individual lot, with no regard for the time of transfer of one lot over another. But an unwritten right (adverse possession, acquiescence, estoppel) that has ripened into a legal right can still override the record — the surveyor discloses it and leaves the legal determination to a court.

content.html:
Other 'treat as a subdivision' transactions

The recorded plat is the paradigm, but the simultaneous principle reaches any instrument that vests several parcels at once:

  • Proceedings in partition. A court decree partitioning co-owned land vests title in the various parties at the same time and by the same decree; none is senior to the other. Treat it as a subdivision.
  • Heirs under a will. All devisees receiving land under a single will take with equal standing and no senior rights. Treat it as a subdivision.

In each case the parcels are of equal dignity and excess/deficiency is apportioned, not assigned to a 'junior.'

content.text: Texas differs / caution: Simultaneous-conveyance doctrine is general American boundary law and Texas applies it without material variation — the recorded subdivision plat is the paradigm, metes-and-bounds carve-outs are the sequential paradigm. But the doctrine is not universal: some states (e.g., Pennsylvania) do not recognize simultaneous conveyances at all. Always confirm the rule of the state where the land lies; relocation of subdivision boundaries must follow that state's laws (federal subdivisions excepted).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A simultaneous boundary is created in the same legal instant, by the same party, by the same instrument — classically a recorded subdivision plat.
The three requirements: divisions shown on a plat, the map filed and approved, and parcels sold by reference to the approved plat.
All simultaneously created parcels are of equal dignity; no lot is senior, and the order of later sale is immaterial.
Partition decrees and devises by will are treated as subdivisions (equal standing, apportionment).
Excess/deficiency is proportioned by frontage, but ripened unwritten rights still prevail.

flashcards.definition: Several parcels created in the same legal instant by the same party and the same instrument (classically the lots of a recorded subdivision plat); all parcels are of equal dignity with no senior rights among them.
The principle that lots created simultaneously have equal standing, so no lot can claim seniority over another; excess and deficiency are apportioned rather than dumped on a 'junior' lot.

flashcards.term: Three requirements for a simultaneous (plat) conveyance
flashcards.definition: (1) all divisions shown on a plat/map, (2) the map filed and approved, (3) parcels sold with reference to the APPROVED plat or map.
A court decree dividing co-owned land that vests title in all parties at the same time and by the same decree; treated as a subdivision (no senior rights, apportion excess/deficiency).

images_needed.caption: A recorded subdivision plat showing a parent parcel divided into numbered lots and blocks with streets, captioned to show all lots created at the same instant (equal dignity).

lessons.title: What Controls a Subdivision Boundary: The Order of Evidence
learning_objectives: State the order of controlling evidence for locating subdivision lot lines.
Distinguish lots created by protraction from lots created by survey and monumentation.
Explain why found original monuments control over the plat record, and how senior rights still bind the exterior boundary.

content.html:
Protraction vs. survey-and-monumentation

How interior lots are located depends on how they were originally created:

  • If interior lots were created by protraction (drawn on paper, never staked on the ground), the lots are located by measurement from the subdivision boundaries according to the plat dimensions.
  • If interior lots were created by survey with monumentation (staked on the ground at the time of platting), then the original survey and its monuments control the lot locations — even over the plat's stated dimensions.

The intentions of the parties to the subdivision are paramount to all other considerations, informed by supplemental clauses in the deeds and by the applicable state subdivision laws.

Original lines are unalterable

Once a lot, street, or block line within a subdivision has been established by the original surveyor and the land is sold in accordance with the original plat, those lines as originally marked and surveyed are unalterable except by resubdivision. A later surveyor is not empowered to place subdivision lines in any position other than those established by the original surveyor; if a later surveyor sets the lines erroneously, the lines must be re-established in their original position. (Brown traces this principle to the federal PLSS rule of the Land Act of February 11, 1805, that original lines and corners are unalterable by surveyors or courts.) The subdivision's exterior boundary is the possible exception — it may still be subject to senior rights of adjoining tracts.

Monuments control the facts on the plat

In a lot-and-block description, the subdivision monuments called for on the plat — or monuments set by others and known to perpetuate the position of the original monuments — control the position of the original lot lines, if they are properly identified and undisturbed. Original monuments set on the ground control the facts given on the plat (unless the intent is clearly otherwise): where the map and the facts on the ground are inconsistent, the map must yield to the ground. A monument's markings, after due allowance for weathering and deterioration, should be substantially the same as the record describing it.

content.text: Not all monuments are original monuments. Monuments that later became 'title monuments' by prescription, agreement, or estoppel cannot be used to determine or control the original lines of the subdivision — they only fix a title boundary between the affected owners. Likewise, uncalled-for monuments cannot be automatically accepted; if they fit the measured distances and directions they may be accepted by 'common report,' but a monument that can positively be proved incorrect cannot be accepted no matter how many people rely on it.

content.html:
The street-line hierarchy and senior rights

For locating street and lot lines, Brown lays out a fallback order: (1) natural monuments called for (a river, shoreline) are presumed to control; (2) then artificial monuments representing the lines actually run by the original surveyor; (3) then improvements (curbs, paving, fences) installed in accordance with the original survey monuments — but only while they were in fact built to those monuments; (4) then the plat width, distances, and angles; and as a last resort, the width scaled from the map. Throughout, original monuments set to control the subdivision boundary yield to senior rights in the event of an overlap, and where a gap exists between the subdivision and an adjoiner, the lines marked and surveyed control over a mere call for the adjoiner. Every rule here is rebuttable by contrary evidence.

headers: Controlling evidence for a subdivision lot/street line
rows: Found original monuments (or reliable perpetuations of them), properly identified and undisturbed
Artificial monuments / lines actually run by the original surveyor
Improvements built in accordance with the original survey monuments
Plat record: width, distances, and angles (proportion excess/deficiency here)
Width scaled from the map (last resort)

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Protracted lots are located by measurement from the subdivision boundary; surveyed-and-monumented lots are located by the original monuments.
Original lines established by the creating surveyor are unalterable except by resubdivision; an erroneous later line must be put back where the original was.
Found original monuments control over the plat; where map and ground disagree, the map yields to the ground.
Order of control: found monuments > natural monuments > artificial monuments/lines run > conforming improvements > plat record > scaled width.
The exterior boundary still yields to the senior rights of adjoining tracts.

flashcards.definition: Creation of lots by drawing them on the plat without staking them on the ground; such lots are located by measurement from the subdivision boundaries per the plat dimensions.

Rule that an undisturbed, properly identified original monument set on the ground controls the position of the lot line over the figures shown on the plat; where map and ground conflict, the map yields to the ground.

Once the original surveyor established a lot/street/block line and land sold by the plat, those lines are unalterable except by resubdivision; an erroneous later line must be re-established in its original position.

Acceptance of a marker as correct because surveyors and the community have long relied on it, even though it cannot be positively proved; a marker proved to be incorrect cannot be accepted by common report.

A title monument fixes a boundary between owners by prescription, agreement, or estoppel; it cannot be used to determine or control the ORIGINAL lines of the subdivision, which only original monuments fix.

images_needed.caption: A subdivision block where a found original iron pipe sits offset from the platted lot-line position, with the boundary held at the found monument (map yields to ground).

lessons.title: Single Proportionate Measurement: Apportioning a Block
learning_objectives: Explain when proportioning applies and why it is a last resort.
Compute the corrected frontage of each lot by single proportionate measurement.
Compute the corrected distance to an interior lot corner using cumulative proportion.

content.html:
When (and why) you proportion

Within a subdivision, distance, direction, and area are subordinate to the intent of the subdivider: the lines are as marked and surveyed, and original monuments control. Excess or deficiency appears when newer, more precise measurements between original monument positions differ from the original record measurements. When no better evidence of the interior corner positions survives, that excess or deficiency is distributed in proportion to the record (linear) measurements between the original found monuments. Proportioning is genuinely a last resort — used only after found monuments, perpetuations, natural/artificial monuments, and conforming improvements have all been exhausted.

Single proportionate measurement

Single (one-dimensional) proportionate measurement restores interior corners on a single straight line between two found controlling monuments. The corrected length of each interval bears the same ratio to the corrected whole as the record interval bears to the record whole. Define the proportion factor k as the measured total divided by the record total; then each lot's corrected frontage is its record frontage times k. When the lots have equal record frontage, every lot simply becomes the measured total divided by the number of lots.

Worked example (Hermansen block)

A 1950 subdivision created six lots, each with a record frontage of 100 ft (record block = 600 ft). A retracement finds the two original concrete block-corner monuments but no interior monuments, and the measured distance between them is 596.44 ft — a deficiency of 3.56 ft. The proportion factor is k = 596.44 / 600 = 0.99407. Each equal lot's corrected frontage is 100 × 0.99407 = 99.41 ft (equivalently 596.44 / 6 = 99.41 ft). The 3.56-ft shortage is shared by all six lots, not dumped on the end lot.

Cumulative proportion: distance to an interior corner

To set an interior corner i lots in from a found end monument, do not multiply lot-by-lot and accumulate rounding — apply the factor to the cumulative record distance. The corrected distance from the found monument to the corner between lot i and lot i+1 is the measured total times the ratio of the cumulative record distance to that corner over the record total.

content.label: Cumulative (single) proportion to interior corner i

content.text: Stop at undisturbed monuments. Excess or deficiency cannot be distributed BEYOND any undisturbed original monument. In the Hermansen example, an original cedar stake survived at the lot 4/5 corner; an honest apportionment must proportion the record 0-to-400 ft segment to the measured distance to that stake, and the record 400-to-600 segment to the remainder — NOT proportion straight across the whole block as if the stake were absent. Failing to use a found interior monument is exactly how two surveyors end up 1.79 ft apart on the same block.

Texas differs (PLSS): 'Single proportionate measurement' is a federal-PLSS/BLM term from the Manual of Surveying Instructions for restoring lost section corners. Texas retained its own public domain at annexation and has no township-range grid, so Texas resolves most retracement by following the original surveyor's footsteps and monuments, with proportioning used sparingly as a fallback. There is no controlling Texas Supreme Court case adopting 'single proportionate measurement' by name; it enters Texas practice through accepted surveying methodology and expert testimony.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Proportion only as a last resort, after found monuments and all better evidence are exhausted.
Single proportion factor k = measured total / record total; corrected lot = k times record lot.
For equal lots, corrected frontage = measured total / number of lots.
Find an interior corner with cumulative proportion: measured total times (cumulative record distance / record total).
Never distribute excess/deficiency across an undisturbed original interior monument — proportion each segment separately.

flashcards.definition: One-dimensional restoration of interior corners on a straight line between two found controlling monuments; each interval's corrected length equals its record length times (measured total / record total).
The ratio of the measured total distance to the record total distance between controlling monuments; multiply each record lot frontage by k to get its corrected frontage.

The difference between the newer measured distance and the original record distance between original monuments; distributed among the lots in proportion to their record measurements when no better evidence exists.

flashcards.term: No distribution beyond an undisturbed monument
flashcards.definition: Rule that excess or deficiency may not be prorated across any found, undisturbed original monument; each segment bounded by such monuments is proportioned independently.
Surveying maxim that proportionate measurement is used only after found monuments, perpetuations, natural and artificial monuments, and conforming improvements have all failed to fix the corner.

images_needed.caption: A block of six 100-ft record lots with the two original concrete end monuments found and a measured total of 596.44 ft, showing each lot corrected to 99.41 ft (record vs measured dimension strip).

lessons.title: Excess, Deficiency, Remnants, and Double Proportion
learning_objectives: Compute the total excess/deficiency in a block and the per-lot adjustment.
Identify when a blunder is placed where it occurs versus prorated, and explain the remnant rule and its dangers.
Describe single-proportion restoration of a lost corner between two found corners, and the concept of double proportionate measurement.

content.html:
Total surplus/shortage and per-lot adjustment

The total excess or deficiency in a block is simply the measured total between controlling monuments minus the record total. When the lots have equal record frontage, the per-lot adjustment is that total divided by the number of lots (added for excess, subtracted for deficiency). For unequal lots the adjustment to each lot is the total surplus times that lot's share of the record total. Either way the corrected frontages must sum back to the measured total — that is the check.

content.label: Excess/deficiency distributed in proportion to record frontage

content.html:
Blunder vs. distributed error

Proportioning assumes the discrepancy is ordinary distributed measurement error. If instead the excess or deficiency was caused by an isolable mathematical error (blunder) in one lot, that error is given to the lot where it occurs — "an error should be placed where the error occurs, if it can be so ascertained." And an error within one block is never prorated into another block: if Block 3 is 1 ft short and Block 2 is 1 ft long, you cannot give Block 2's extra foot to Block 3. Excess or deficiency in the width of a street is normally not prorated either (though not all courts agree).

The remnant rule and the end lot

Two end-lot rules matter. First, where the end lot's frontage is not given on the plat (the interior lots carry the dimensions), all of the excess or deficiency is presumed to fall on that end lot. Second, where the surplus or shortage is deliberately thrown onto an irregularly shaped lot at the end of the block, the method is the remnant rule — accepted in few jurisdictions, and a trap for the unwary surveyor (Brown points to Barrett v. Perkins, 113 Minn. 480 (1911)). A considerable excess/deficiency in blocks adjoining a subdivision boundary is prorated only as a last resort, and any leftover is given to the lot on the subdivision boundary — it is normally safer to locate lots from the interior of a subdivision than from its outer boundary.

Single-proportion restoration of one lost corner

To restore a single lost corner that lies between two found corners on a line, proportion the record distances to the measured distance. If a lost corner has record distances a and b to the two found monuments (record span a+b) and the measured span between those monuments is m, the corrected distance from the first found monument to the lost corner is m × a / (a+b). The same ratio idea, applied to a single straight line, is the one-dimensional case you will use most often inside a block.

content.label: Single-proportion position of a lost corner between two found corners

content.html:
Double proportionate measurement (concept)

When a lost corner is controlled in two directions — as a PLSS section corner common to four sections is — it is restored by double proportionate measurement: proportion independently along the latitudinal (E–W) line between the nearest found corners and along the longitudinal (N–S) line between the nearest found corners, then bring the two proportioned positions together to fix the corner (with a small balancing of the differences). This is a federal BLM / PLSS construct for restoring lost public-land corners, not a routine subdivision tool. In a lot-and-block subdivision the one-dimensional single-proportion method along a line is almost always what applies.

content.text: Texas differs (PLSS): Double proportionate measurement comes from the BLM Manual of Surveying Instructions and presupposes the federal township-range grid. Texas land was never federal public domain — it has no PLSS grid — so double proportion is a borrowed fallback with no marquee Texas case adopting it. In Texas, a 'lost' corner is restored first from the original surveyor's footsteps and the GLO original-survey record; proportioning of any kind is used only when those fail. Do not attribute a famous Texas case to double proportionate measurement.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Total Δ = measured total − record total; per-lot adjustment is Δ times the lot's share of the record total (Δ/n for equal lots).
An isolable blunder is placed in the lot where it occurs; error in one block is never prorated into another, and street width is normally not prorated.
If the end lot's dimension is omitted it absorbs the excess/deficiency; the remnant rule throws surplus onto an irregular end lot but is disfavored.
A single lost corner between two found corners is restored by m × a/(a+b) along the line.
Double proportionate measurement (two-direction restoration) is a BLM/PLSS method; in Texas it is a fallback with no controlling case.

flashcards.definition: Method of throwing all of a block's excess or deficiency onto an irregularly shaped lot at the end of the block; accepted in few jurisdictions and a recognized trap for surveyors.
Rule that an excess or deficiency traceable to a mathematical blunder in one particular lot is assigned to that lot, rather than prorated among all lots, if the error can be ascertained.

BLM/PLSS method for restoring a lost corner controlled in two directions, proportioning independently along the E–W and N–S lines between the nearest found corners; not a routine subdivision tool and not adopted by a marquee Texas case.

An original corner whose position cannot be recovered from the original monument or any acceptable collateral evidence, so it must be restored by proportionate measurement; contrast an obliterated corner, which is recoverable.
Where the plat omits the end lot's dimension and gives only the interior lot dimensions, all of the block's excess or deficiency is presumed to fall on the end lot.

images_needed.caption: A line between two found monuments with a lost interior corner, record distances a and b shown above and the proportioned position m·a/(a+b) shown below.
A PLSS-style lost section corner restored by double proportion: four surrounding found corners, with E-W and N-S proportioned positions converging on the restored corner.

lessons.title: Rules of Construction for Combined Descriptions
learning_objectives: Apply the construction rules for 'part-of-a-lot' (an 'of') descriptions in subdivisions and metes-and-bounds tracts.
Apply the construction rules for fractional ('half') conveyances and for dividing lots into halves.
State good-practice rules for writing combined descriptions.

content.html:
Combined and 'part-of-a-lot' descriptions

Subdivision practice constantly produces combination descriptions — a deed conveys a portion of a platted lot, or a portion of a metes-and-bounds tract, by reference to the larger parcel plus added measurements. Brown supplies presumptions for reading these 'an of' descriptions:

  • Conveying a portion of a subdivision lot, the owner conveys the part called for measuring from the side line of the street (the original lot line). Vacations or openings of streets do not alter the original lot lines.
  • Conveying a portion of a metes-and-bounds tract, measurements run from the boundary line, even where that boundary line lies within a street.
  • When the 'of' description is given by lineal measurement, the presumption is that the measurement is made at right angles to the boundary line it runs from, unless otherwise specified.

Fractions and dividing a lot into halves

Fractional conveyances follow their own presumptions:

  • When a fraction of the whole is conveyed — e.g., 'the west half' — the conveyance is presumed to be based on area measurement unless stated otherwise.
  • When the easterly and westerly lot lines are shown parallel on the map and are in fact nearly parallel, the dividing line between the east and west halves is run on the mean bearing of the two side lines.
  • When those side lines are not parallel (and not cardinal), the dividing line between east and west halves is made to run north and south.
  • Where a deed conveys the 'east one-half of lot 1' and a companion deed conveys 'lot 1, except the east one-half,' the west line of the east half is commonly taken to be parallel with the east line of the lot, provided that east line is nearly cardinal.

rows: Measure from the side line of the street (original lot line); street vacations don't move it
Measure from the boundary line, even if it lies in a street
Measurement is at right angles to the boundary it runs from, unless specified
Based on area, unless stated otherwise
Divide on the mean bearing of the two side lines
Side lines not parallel / not cardinal

content.html:
Laying off a given area

When a stated area must be laid off without other qualifying terms: an area on the side of a tract is taken as a parallelogram; an area laid off on a given line is presumed a square; and an area laid off in a corner of a parcel is presumed to be as nearly a parallelogram with equal sides as the circumstances permit. These presumptions only fill gaps — express terms in the deed always govern.

content.text: Good drafting practice. The scrivener should write each description as an individual creation, assuming a continuity of words and definitions throughout. Avoid deed calls measured from two different directions in the same description — they breed gaps, overlaps, and ambiguity. These are rules of construction (gap-fillers): the controlling rule remains the expressed intent of the parties read from the whole instrument.

Texas differs / order of authority: These construction presumptions are general American boundary-law canons and Texas applies them, but in Texas they are strictly SUBORDINATE. Texas construes a deed by the intent expressed within the four corners (Stafford v. King; Bass v. Harper), follows the original surveyor's footsteps, and applies the dignity of calls (natural > artificial > course > distance > quantity) before resorting to mechanical default canons. Use these presumptions only to fill genuine gaps the instrument leaves open.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A portion 'of' a subdivision lot is measured from the street side line; a portion 'of' a metes-and-bounds tract from the boundary line — even in a street.
An 'of' lineal measurement is presumed at right angles to the boundary unless specified.
A 'west half' is presumed by area; divide parallel-sided lots on the mean bearing, non-parallel lots north–south.
Laid-off areas default to a parallelogram (side), a square (on a line), or near-equal-sided parallelogram (corner).
All such canons are gap-fillers, subordinate in Texas to expressed intent, footsteps, and the dignity of calls.

flashcards.definition: A description conveying a portion of a larger parcel by reference to it plus added measurements; measured from the street side line for a subdivision lot, or from the boundary line for a metes-and-bounds tract.
When a fraction of the whole (e.g., 'the west half') is conveyed, the division is presumed based on area unless the deed states otherwise.

When a lot's side lines are shown and are in fact nearly parallel, the line dividing the lot into halves is run on the mean (average) bearing of the two side lines; if the sides are not parallel, the dividing line runs north–south.

Absent qualifying terms, a given area on the side of a tract is a parallelogram, an area on a given line is a square, and an area in a corner is as near an equal-sided parallelogram as circumstances permit.

A default interpretive presumption used only to fill gaps the instrument leaves open; subordinate to the expressed intent of the parties, the footsteps of the original surveyor, and the dignity of calls.

images_needed.caption: A trapezoidal lot with non-parallel east and west side lines, showing the north–south dividing line splitting it into east and west halves, contrasted with a parallel-sided lot split on the mean bearing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes a 'simultaneously created' boundary?
options: Several parcels created in the same legal instant, by the same party, by the same instrument
Two adjoining tracts carved out of a parent tract several years apart
A boundary fixed by long acquiescence between two neighbors
A boundary established by adverse possession over the statutory period
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Several parcels created in the same legal instant, by the same party, by the same instrument
quiz_questions.explanation: A simultaneous conveyance creates several parcels at one legal instant by one instrument (the recorded plat is the paradigm), giving every parcel equal dignity. Carving tracts out years apart is sequential (senior/junior rights). Acquiescence and adverse possession are unwritten-rights doctrines, not methods of original simultaneous creation.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a recorded subdivision, the lot that was sold first is senior to the lots sold later and takes its full record dimension before the others are adjusted.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: All lots in a recorded subdivision are created at the same instant and have equal standing; the order in which lots later sell is immaterial to seniority. Excess and deficiency are apportioned by frontage, not assigned by sale order.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three requirements Brown gives for a simultaneous (plat) conveyance?
options: Each lot must be physically monumented before any lot can be sold
All divisions of the land are indicated and shown on a plat or map
The map is filed and approved
Parcels are sold with reference to the approved plat or map
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Each lot must be physically monumented before any lot can be sold
quiz_questions.explanation: The three requirements are: divisions shown on a plat, the map filed and approved, and parcels sold by reference to the approved plat. Monumentation affects HOW lots are later located (survey-and-monument vs. protraction) but is not one of the three threshold requirements for a simultaneous conveyance to exist.

quiz_questions.question_text: A court decree of partition divides co-owned land among four owners. How are these resulting parcels treated for boundary-resolution purposes?
options: As a subdivision — equal dignity, excess/deficiency apportioned
Sequentially, with the first-named party in the decree senior
As sequential conveyances ranked by deed-recording date
Each parcel is independent and never apportioned
quiz_questions.correct_answer: As a subdivision — equal dignity, excess/deficiency apportioned
quiz_questions.explanation: A partition decree vests title in all parties at the same time by the same decree, so none is senior. Brown directs that it be treated as a subdivision (equal dignity, apportionment). The same is true for heirs taking under a single will.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: Every state recognizes simultaneous conveyances and applies proportionate apportionment to subdivision lots.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Some states (Hermansen notes Pennsylvania) do not recognize simultaneous conveyances at all. Relocation of subdivision boundaries must follow the law of the state where the land lies, so the surveyor must confirm local rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Interior lots of a subdivision were created by protraction (drawn on the plat, never staked). How are those lot lines located on the ground?
options: By measurement from the subdivision boundaries per the plat dimensions
By the original interior monuments, which always exist for protracted lots
By scaling the recorded map regardless of the stated dimensions
By splitting the difference between adjoining deeds
quiz_questions.correct_answer: By measurement from the subdivision boundaries per the plat dimensions
quiz_questions.explanation: Protracted lots were never monumented, so they are located by measurement from the subdivision boundaries using the plat dimensions. Only lots created by survey-and-monumentation are located by their original interior monuments. Scaling is a last resort used when dimensions are missing.

quiz_questions.question_text: An undisturbed original iron pipe is found 0.4 ft off the position computed from the plat dimensions. What controls the lot corner?
options: The found original monument — where the map and the ground disagree, the map yields to the ground
The plat dimension, because recorded figures are more precise than a weathered pipe
The average of the monument and the computed position
Whichever the current owner prefers
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The found original monument — where the map and the ground disagree, the map yields to the ground
quiz_questions.explanation: An undisturbed, properly identified original monument controls the position of the original lot line over the figures shown on the plat. The map must yield to the facts on the ground.

quiz_questions.question_text: A later surveyor discovers that a still-earlier surveyor placed a subdivision lot line in the wrong position relative to the original surveyor's line. What is the correct treatment?
options: Re-establish the line in its original position; a later surveyor cannot relocate the original line
Hold the later surveyor's erroneous line because it was set in good faith
Split the difference between the two surveyors' lines
Prorate the discrepancy across the whole block
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Re-establish the line in its original position; a later surveyor cannot relocate the original line
quiz_questions.explanation: Once the original surveyor establishes a line and land is sold by the plat, the line is unalterable except by resubdivision. A later surveyor is not empowered to relocate it; an erroneously set line must be put back where the original surveyor ran it.

quiz_questions.question_text: A fence between two lots became a recognized title boundary through years of acquiescence, but it does not match the original lot line. May the surveyor use that fence to determine the ORIGINAL subdivision lines?
options: No — a monument that became a title monument by acquiescence/estoppel cannot fix the original subdivision lines
Yes — any long-accepted fence becomes an original monument
Yes, but only if both owners sign a boundary agreement
No, because fences can never affect a boundary in any way
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No — a monument that became a title monument by acquiescence/estoppel cannot fix the original subdivision lines
quiz_questions.explanation: Monuments that became 'title monuments' by prescription, agreement, or estoppel fix a title boundary between the affected owners but cannot be used to determine or control the ORIGINAL lines of the subdivision. Only original monuments (or reliable perpetuations) fix the original lines.

quiz_questions.question_text: The original monuments of a subdivision boundary line overlap a senior adjoining tract. Which controls in the overlap?
options: The senior adjoiner — the subdivision's monuments yield to senior rights in an overlap
The subdivision monuments, because original monuments always control
The overlap is split evenly between the two owners
The junior subdivision, because its plat was recorded
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The senior adjoiner — the subdivision's monuments yield to senior rights in an overlap
quiz_questions.explanation: Original monuments set to control a subdivision boundary still yield to senior rights in the event of an overlap. (In a GAP between the subdivision and an adjoiner, by contrast, the lines marked and surveyed control over a mere call for the adjoiner.)

quiz_questions.question_text: Proportionate measurement should be the surveyor's first choice for locating any interior lot corner in a subdivision.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Proportioning is a last resort. Found original monuments, reliable perpetuations, natural and artificial monuments, and conforming improvements all control ahead of proportionate measurement. You proportion only when no better evidence of the interior corners survives.

quiz_questions.question_text: A block of 5 lots, each with record frontage 60.00 ft (record total 300.00 ft), is measured between found original block-corner monuments at 301.40 ft. Using single proportionate measurement, what is the corrected frontage of each (equal) lot, in feet?
quiz_questions.explanation: Proportion factor k = 301.40 / 300.00 = 1.004667. Each lot = 60.00 × 1.004667 = 60.28 ft (equivalently 301.40 / 5 = 60.28 ft). The 1.40-ft surplus is shared across all five lots.

quiz_questions.question_text: A block has 6 equal record lots of 100.00 ft (record total 600.00 ft) between two found end monuments measured at 596.44 ft. What is the corrected distance from the first found monument to the corner between lot 2 and lot 3, in feet?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cumulative record distance to the lot 2/3 corner = 200.00 ft. Corrected distance = 596.44 × (200.00 / 600.00) = 596.44 × 0.33333 = 198.81 ft. Use the cumulative proportion to avoid accumulating per-lot rounding.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why must a surveyor proportion each segment separately when an undisturbed original interior monument is found partway along the block?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because excess or deficiency cannot be distributed across (beyond) an undisturbed original monument; the found monument fixes that intermediate point, so each segment it bounds is proportioned independently.
quiz_questions.explanation: Excess/deficiency cannot be distributed beyond any undisturbed original monument. The found interior monument is controlling evidence that fixes its corner; proportioning straight across it (ignoring it) is the error that put the 1970 and 2001 surveyors 1.79 ft apart in the Hermansen example.

quiz_questions.question_text: Between two found original monuments the record distance is 500.00 ft but the measured distance is 498.00 ft. A lot has a record frontage of 50.00 ft. What is its corrected frontage, in feet?
quiz_questions.explanation: k = 498.00 / 500.00 = 0.9960. Corrected frontage = 50.00 × 0.9960 = 49.80 ft. The 2.00-ft deficiency is distributed in proportion to record frontage.

quiz_questions.question_text: A block of 8 equal record lots of 50.00 ft (record total 400.00 ft) measures 403.20 ft between found end monuments. What is the per-lot adjustment (added to each record frontage), in feet?
quiz_questions.explanation: Total excess Δ = 403.20 − 400.00 = 3.20 ft. Per-lot adjustment = Δ / n = 3.20 / 8 = 0.40 ft. Each corrected lot = 50.40 ft, and 8 × 50.40 = 403.20 ft (check).

quiz_questions.question_text: A block is measured 1.0 ft long overall, but the surveyor determines the entire 1.0 ft came from a single mis-recorded interior lot dimension. What is the correct treatment?
options: Place the 1.0 ft in the lot where the error occurs; do not prorate it across the block
Prorate the 1.0 ft equally among all lots in the block
Give the entire 1.0 ft to the end lot under the remnant rule
Prorate the 1.0 ft into the adjoining block
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Place the 1.0 ft in the lot where the error occurs; do not prorate it across the block
quiz_questions.explanation: An error traceable to a blunder in one lot is placed where it occurs ('an error should be placed where the error occurs, if it can be so ascertained'). Proportioning assumes ordinary distributed error; an isolable blunder is not smeared across all lots, and never into another block.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'remnant rule' — throwing all excess or deficiency onto an irregularly shaped lot at the end of a block — is widely accepted and is the preferred method in most jurisdictions.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: The remnant rule is accepted in FEW jurisdictions and Brown flags it as a trap for the unwary surveyor (Barrett v. Perkins). Proportioning by record frontage, not the remnant rule, is the general approach.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lost corner lies on a line between two found corners, with record distances of 300.00 ft and 200.00 ft to the two found monuments (record span 500.00 ft). The measured span between the found monuments is 505.00 ft. What is the corrected distance from the first found monument to the lost corner, in feet?
quiz_questions.explanation: Single-proportion: X = m × a/(a+b) = 505.00 × 300.00/500.00 = 505.00 × 0.60 = 303.00 ft. The 5.00-ft surplus is split in proportion to the record distances (3.00 ft to the first segment, 2.00 ft to the second).

quiz_questions.question_text: Double proportionate measurement is primarily a method for which of the following?
options: Restoring a lost PLSS corner controlled in two directions from four surrounding found corners
Dividing a subdivision lot into an east half and a west half
Distributing street-width excess among abutting lots
Determining senior rights between two sequential deeds
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Restoring a lost PLSS corner controlled in two directions from four surrounding found corners
quiz_questions.explanation: Double proportionate measurement is the BLM/PLSS method for a lost corner controlled in two directions, proportioning independently along the E–W and N–S lines between the nearest found corners. The other options are unrelated to it.

quiz_questions.question_text: When a subdivision plat gives dimensions for the interior lots but omits the dimension of the end lot, where is the block's excess or deficiency presumed to fall?
options: Entirely on the end lot whose dimension was omitted
Equally across all lots including the end lot
On the two lots nearest the block corners
It is given to the street
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Entirely on the end lot whose dimension was omitted
quiz_questions.explanation: When the end lot's measurement is not given, all of the excess or deficiency is presumed to fall on that end lot — the interior lots hold their stated dimensions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed conveys 'the west 40 feet of Lot 7' in a recorded subdivision. From where is the 40 feet measured?
options: From the side line of the street (the original lot line); street vacations do not move it
From the centerline of the street fronting the lot
From the nearest set monument regardless of the lot line
From the geographic center of Lot 7
quiz_questions.correct_answer: From the side line of the street (the original lot line); street vacations do not move it
quiz_questions.explanation: For a portion 'of' a subdivision lot, the measurement runs from the side line of the street — the original lot line. Vacations or openings of streets do not alter the original lot lines from which the measurement is made.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lot's easterly and westerly side lines are NOT parallel and not cardinal. A deed conveys 'the east half' of the lot. How is the dividing line drawn?
options: It is made to run north and south
It is run on the mean bearing of the two side lines
It bisects the angle between the two side lines
It runs perpendicular to the front lot line
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It is made to run north and south
quiz_questions.explanation: When the side lines are not parallel (and not cardinal), the line dividing the lot into east and west halves is made to run north and south. The mean-bearing rule applies only when the side lines are shown parallel and are in fact nearly parallel.

quiz_questions.question_text: Absent qualifying terms, a stated area to be laid off 'on a given line' is presumed to take what shape?
options: A square
A parallelogram on the side of the tract
A near-equal-sided parallelogram in a corner
A circle of equal area
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A square
quiz_questions.explanation: An area laid off on a given line is presumed a square; an area on the side of a tract is a parallelogram; an area in a corner is as near an equal-sided parallelogram as circumstances permit. These are gap-filling presumptions only.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Texas, the rules of construction for combined descriptions (the 'of' and 'half' presumptions) override the expressed intent of the parties and the dignity of calls.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: These canons are gap-fillers and are strictly subordinate. Texas construes a deed by the intent expressed within its four corners (Stafford v. King; Bass v. Harper), follows the original surveyor's footsteps, and applies the dignity of calls before resorting to mechanical default presumptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does Brown advise against writing a description with calls measured from two different directions?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because two-direction calls breed gaps, overlaps, and ambiguity; each description should be written as an individual creation with a continuity of words and definitions.
quiz_questions.explanation: Calls run from two different directions in one description create internal conflicts (gaps and overlaps) that later surveyors must resolve. Good drafting writes each description as an individual creation assuming continuity of words and definitions.

problem_templates.name: Single proportionate distribution — corrected lot frontage
problem_templates.question_template: A block of {{n}} equal lots was platted, each with a record frontage of {{rec:f2}} ft (record total {{recTotal:f2}} ft). A resurvey finds the actual measured distance between the two original found block-corner monuments is {{meas:f2}} ft. Using single proportionate measurement, what is the corrected frontage (ft) of each lot?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Single proportionate measurement distributes the {{surplus:f2}} ft excess/deficiency in proportion to record frontage. For equal lots the corrected frontage is the measured total divided by the number of lots: {{meas:f2}} ÷ {{n}} = {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Block of n equal record lots with the two original end monuments found and a measured total differing from record.

problem_templates.name: Cumulative proportion — distance to an interior lot corner
problem_templates.question_template: A block has {{n}} equal record lots of {{rec:f2}} ft each (record total {{recTotal:f2}} ft) between two found original end monuments measured at {{meas:f2}} ft. By single proportionate measurement, what is the corrected distance (ft) from the first found monument to the corner between lot {{i}} and lot {{i}}+1?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Use cumulative proportion to avoid accumulating per-lot rounding: corrected distance = measured total × (cumulative record distance ÷ record total) = {{meas:f2}} × ({{cumRec:f2}} ÷ {{recTotal:f2}}) = {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Block with the corrected distance from a found end monument to an interior lot corner i lots in.

problem_templates.name: Excess/deficiency — total surplus and per-lot adjustment
problem_templates.question_template: A block of {{n}} equal record lots of {{rec:f2}} ft each (record total {{recTotal:f2}} ft) is measured at {{meas:f2}} ft between found original monuments. What is the per-lot adjustment (ft) that must be added to (or subtracted from) each record frontage?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Total excess/deficiency Δ = {{meas:f2}} − {{recTotal:f2}} = {{delta:f2}} ft. For equal lots the per-lot adjustment is Δ ÷ n = {{delta:f2}} ÷ {{n}} = {{_answer}} ft (positive = add to each lot, negative = subtract).

images_needed.caption: Record vs measured block total with the surplus/shortage labeled and distributed per lot.

problem_templates.name: Single-proportion restoration of a lost corner between two found corners
problem_templates.question_template: A lost corner lies on a straight line between two found original corners. Its record distances to the two found monuments are {{a:f2}} ft and {{b:f2}} ft (record span {{recSpan:f2}} ft). The measured span between the found monuments is {{m:f2}} ft. By single proportionate measurement, what is the corrected distance (ft) from the first found monument to the lost corner?

solution_steps_template.title: Lost-corner distance
solution_steps_template.result_template: = {{_answer}} ft from the first found monument

problem_templates.explanation_template: Single-proportion restoration: X = m × a/(a+b) = {{m:f2}} × {{a:f2}} ÷ {{recSpan:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft. The {{spanDelta:f2}} ft surplus/shortage in the span is split in proportion to the record distances a and b.

images_needed.caption: Line between two found monuments with record distances a and b to a lost corner and the proportioned position below.

homework.title: Homework 5A — Apportioning the Hermansen Block
homework.prompt:
A 1950 subdivision created **six lots, each 100 ft wide** (record block = 600 ft), with the two block corners monumented by concrete monuments and each interior lot corner by a cedar stake. Work the following, showing all computations and a short written justification for each step.

1. **No interior monuments found.** A 1970 retracement finds only the two original concrete end monuments; the measured distance between them is **596.44 ft**. Apportion the block by single proportionate measurement: give the corrected frontage of each lot and the corrected distance from the west end monument to each interior lot corner. Confirm your corrected frontages sum to 596.44 ft.
2. **One interior monument survives.** Now suppose the 1970 surveyor also found the **original cedar stake at the lot 4/5 corner**, measured 395.23 ft from the west concrete monument (and 201.21 ft to the east monument). Re-apportion: proportion the record 0–400 ft segment to 395.23 ft and the record 400–600 ft segment to 201.21 ft. Give the corrected frontage of every lot.
3. **Compare and explain.** State the corrected lot 4 width from part 1 versus part 2, and explain in 3–5 sentences why ignoring the found cedar stake (part 1) produced a different answer — and which answer is correct under the rule that excess/deficiency cannot be distributed beyond an undisturbed original monument.
4. **Documentation.** In 2–3 sentences, explain what the 1970 surveyor should have recorded (per Hermansen) so that a later surveyor would not reject the set monuments, and name two acceptable recording vehicles.

images_needed.caption: Six-lot block: top shows record 100-ft lots; middle shows the 596.44-ft measured span with only the two concrete end monuments; bottom shows the surviving cedar stake at the 4/5 corner at 395.23 ft / 201.21 ft.

homework.title: Homework 5B — Construction of Combined Descriptions (written)
homework.prompt:
Answer each in a short paragraph, citing the controlling rule of construction and (where relevant) the paramount Texas principle that intent and the original surveyor's footsteps govern.

1. A deed conveys 'the **east 50 feet of Lot 12**' of a recorded subdivision; the street in front of Lot 12 was later vacated. From what line is the 50 ft measured, and does the vacation change the answer? Why?
2. A grantor conveys 'the **west half of Lot 9**.' The lot's east and west side lines are shown parallel on the plat and are in fact nearly parallel. How is the dividing line drawn? How would your answer change if the side lines were clearly **not** parallel and not cardinal?
3. A description requires laying off **two acres in the northeast corner** of a larger tract, with no qualifying terms. What shape is presumed, and why is this only a gap-filling presumption?
4. **Texas overlay.** Explain in 4–6 sentences why these 'of' and 'half' presumptions are subordinate in Texas, identifying the order of authority a Texas court applies (expressed intent within the four corners, footsteps of the original surveyor, dignity of calls) before reaching a mechanical default canon. Note that Texas, having retained its public domain at annexation, has no PLSS grid, so PLSS-style double proportionate measurement is a borrowed fallback rather than a controlling Texas rule.
